---
title: "Day 16 — WaveNet, and reading torch.nn without flinching"
subtitle: "Saturday, 8 August 2026 · Week 3 · Tokens and attention · Study day · 3-4 h"
---

::: {.day-nav .kind-study}
[← Day 15](day-15.qmd) · [All 50 days](../timeline.qmd) · [Day 17 →](day-17.qmd)
:::

::: {.why}
Two loose ends close today. The WaveNet lecture turns the flat MLP into a hierarchical model that fuses characters in pairs instead of crushing all eight at once, and on the way it rebuilds Linear, BatchNorm1d, Embedding, Tanh and Sequential as classes short enough to read in one sitting. SLP3 Ch6 is the same material as a textbook, which is the cheapest way to find whatever the videos skipped past.
:::

## What you should be able to do tonight

- You can say what FlattenConsecutive(2) does to a (32, 8, 10) tensor and why three of them reduce a context of 8 to one vector.
- You can open torch/nn/modules/module.py and point at the code that lets model.parameters() find a parameter nested three levels down.
- You can name the BatchNorm bug Karpathy hits with 3-D input and say what shape bnmean had before the fix versus after.

## Tasks <span class='task-total'>210 min</span>

::: {.task-list}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [60 min]{.task-min} Watch makemore part 5 end to end, tracking one thing above all else: the shape printed after each layer when he loops over model.layers.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [60 min]{.task-min} Build the hierarchical model yourself in makemore_part5_cnn1.ipynb and log your validation loss after each change - flat MLP at context 3, flat at context 8, hierarchical with three FlattenConsecutive(2) layers, then BatchNorm dims fixed. Four numbers, written down, in order.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [60 min]{.task-min} Read SLP3 Ch6 Neural Networks. The feedforward neural language model section is makemore part 2 written as equations - read it as a check on what you already built, and slow down on the computation-graph and backward-differentiation sections, which is yesterday in textbook form.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [30 min]{.task-min} Open the real torch/nn source and read four things: Module.__setattr__ (how assigning a submodule registers it), _named_members behind parameters(), Sequential.forward (a for loop), and Module.train() setting self.training recursively - which is the flag BatchNorm reads to choose batch statistics over running statistics.
:::
:::

## Resources

- [Karpathy L6 - makemore part 5, WaveNet (56m)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t3YJ5hKiMQ0) [video]{.res-kind}
- [nn-zero-to-hero repo - lectures/makemore/makemore_part5_cnn1.ipynb](https://github.com/karpathy/nn-zero-to-hero) [repo]{.res-kind}
- [SLP3 Ch6 - Neural Networks](https://web.stanford.edu/~jurafsky/slp3/6.pdf) [textbook]{.res-kind}
- [PyTorch docs (for nn.Module and nn.Sequential)](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/index.html) [docs]{.res-kind}

## Deliverable

::: {.deliverable}
A working hierarchical model in makemore_part5_cnn1.ipynb with four validation losses logged in order, plus five lines in your notes on what nn.Sequential.forward actually contains and what Module.train() actually changes.
:::

## Checkpoint

::: {.checkpoint}
Batch 32, block size 8, embedding dim 10, 68 channels. Give the tensor shape going into and coming out of each of the three FlattenConsecutive(2) layers, and for each stage say which axes BatchNorm1d has to average over to get the statistics right.
:::

## Prepare for tomorrow

Ten minutes tonight so Day 17, Sunday, 9 August 2026 starts clean. This is what the evening email sends you.

::: {.task-list .prep}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Download SLP3 Ch2 (2.pdf).
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> pip install tiktoken and confirm tiktoken.get_encoding('cl100k_base').encode('hello') returns something.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Write down ten strings from your own work to tokenize tomorrow - a couple of rsIDs, gene and protein symbols, a p-value in scientific notation, a dosage, one sentence that is not English.
:::
:::
